/projects/prepared-location.title: Prepared Map Dashboard | Justin Luo
p:
By creating a map dashboard as the default landing experience, our users were able to immediately see value in Prepared's platform and gain high-level context of their entire jurisidiction.

As a design engineer on this project, I prototyped the feature with real code and data, hoping to gain insight into customer usage and rally internal stakeholders.

After hearing about Cursor AI within the front-end community, I wanted to further develop my design engineering skills, especially since I had the support of the engineering team and codebase access.

I believe the best way to learn is by doing, so I wanted to bring one of my shelved design ideas to life!

In Prepared, users could already see all of their calls in the call list. However, you needed to click on each call to view the call's transcript, summary, location, and other Prepared features.

From observing customers, we found that even that extra click was enough friction to not use Prepared, especially when call takers were already doing so many things for a call.
Users needed to open each call manually

The initial idea was simple – plot all of the recent and active calls on a map of a center's jurisdiction as the home page. Now, users would immediately see valuable location data from the get-go.

The designs received positive feedback from customers and team members, but the idea was not prioritized for several months due to shifting business priorities.
Call preview when clicking on a call marker
Quickly open Street View for call

Even as the company pivoted the team's focus away from location features, I strongly believed in the idea because of its clear improvements for users' workflows within Prepared.

With Cursor by my side, I was able to create a working prototype with live data in 2 days. My goal was to rally internal team members and validate the feature with beta customers.
Working code prototype with test data

After an expedited internal roadshow, I was able to work with the engineering team to get the feature released to production behind a feature flag. 

At the same time, I worked with my PM counterpart to set up a beta group of customers. We were able to get 6 centers signed up by the end of the week, and they were incredibly excited to start using the feature.

In just a few days, we started seeing regular usage from multiple users and tons of feedback on how to improve the experience.

As we received strong positive signals from beta customers, I met with the tech leads and other PDE leadership to prioritize the map dashboard in Q1 of 2025, dedicating a team to handle the upcoming GA release.

We also explored various future design iterations to address customer feedback. One concept that was well-received was a dedicated preview pane that added functionality like call transcript and audio playback. We'll continue to iterate based on customer feedback.
An exploration using a preview pane to display more information

/projects/prepared.title: Prepared | Justin Luo
p: I spent two years with the talented folks at Prepared in NYC. We focused on delivering much needed innovation in 911 technology, building audio processing tools for calls and radio, an analytics dashboard platform, and an automated voice agent for handling admin calls.

Before I joined, our product allowed call takers to text with and get media from callers. What we constantly found was that call takers were overworked, overwhelmed, and ultimately needed help processing incoming 911 data.

To help these mission-critical heroes, we built audio transcription and playback, summary insights, voice translators, and more – all powered by the latest LLM and voice technologies.
Map dashboard to view all calls

Soon after releasing our call taking product, we heard from another persona that needed similar assistive technology – radio dispatchers.

Monitoring radio traffic with responders was incredibly difficult. We brought the same transcription and LLM technology to radio, crafting an intuitive solution to help dispatchers stay on top of radio communications.

Some of our most passionate users were supervisors and QA specialists. They loved the ability to quickly review calls using our transcript and summary features.

As we dove deeper into their experiences, we found opportunities to improve their analytics and QA abilities. From here, we built a product designed for supervisors, including custom dashboards, embedded QA forms, and in-platform feedback experiences.
Robust filtering system with a combobox menu

More than 70% of the calls that 911 centers receive are admin calls - requests for information, transfers to other departments, etc.

At Prepared, we believe that call takers should be focused on the calls that matter. With the latest AI voice technology, we built a voice agent that could naturally handle admin calls, while always transferring to a human operator when there is an actual emergency.
Live monitoring an automated voice agent

One of my proudest achievements at Prepared was helping to scale the product from a novel tool for texting and media, to a mission-critical call-taking assistant, to finally an end-to-end platform for 911 centers.

As our product scaled, so did our design framework. But we maintained a high bar for craft throughout this evolution that continously delivered exceptional customer and business value.
Product expansion during my time at Prepared

As soon as I started at Prepared, I began working with our engineering team to build our design system Prepared UI.  

We formed a UX "guild" to evolve our design system and ensure consistency across our interfaces. And I even got my hands dirty, diving right into the codebase with my engineering peers :)
Not bad for a designer, right?

Evolving our product meant that we needed to evolve our brand. Working alongside the marketing and operations teams, I helped define our new visual language and marketing site design, focusing on making our brand more modern and highlighting our new product focus.

911 is a close-knit community, and we're incredibly grateful to our customers for their ongoing feedback. I had the unique opportunity to lead a project focused on building an integrated online community.

In the community, customers were able to share use-cases with each other, discuss trends in 911, and learn about the latest features. By creating this space, our team was able to deepen our connection to our customers and gain invaluable insight into their experiences.

I am forever grateful for the opportunity to work at Prepared alongside such great team members. We have accomplished so much in such a short time, and I strongly believe that we have expedited innovation in the 911 industry. 

Prepared taught me so much about building products that people love and using a vigorous process to test and iterate on product and design ideas.

Finally, to our customers and users - thank you so much for the life-saving work you do every day to make our communities safer.

/projects/teams-connect.title: Teams Connect | Justin Luo
p:
How might we...

Connect Teams users through chat in a secure way, no matter which account type they are using

Before this project, Teams allowed users to chat with coworkers interally, as well as external collaborators at other organizations.

In 2020, Teams for personal use was released and allowed customers to chat and video call with friends, family, or anyone else using a personal account.

Microsoft then announced that Teams Chat for personal use would come pre-installed with the new Windows 11, with the potential to reach over 1 billion existing Windows customers.
Daniela can chat internally with Aadi or externally with Ray, who works at Fabrikam
Before this project, Teams users on work accounts could only chat with others using work accounts, and Teams personal accounts could only chat with other personal accounts.

With the vast majority of existing Teams users on work accounts, we wanted to connect our existing user base of 250 million monthly active users (MAU) with potential personal accounts to help drive consumer adoption and value.

We saw an opportunity to build chat features between  Teams work and personal accounts to: 

✔  Help Teams users connect with anyone in the world, regardless of account type
✔  Increase usage and adoption of Teams for personal use

This project would also enable several valuable chat scenarios for our existing customers including:

As the lead designer on desktop for this feature, I had to build all of the various flows and their complexities for Teams Connect Chat, while balancing simplicity and alignment across existing chat patterns.

How might we align and consolidate chat headers to account for new scenarios?

As our work connected users to new account “types” and scenarios, we needed to revisit our chat header patterns.

How might we help users feel secure when chatting with new people?

Even with our auto-spam detection, account verification, and IT admin controls, we wanted to take additional steps in the UX to help our chat users feel secure.

When receiving a new message from a user who is outside your organization or not a trusted contact, we designed a “safety” screen that gave three options:

We also worked with our IT admin and chat services teams to:

1) Allow admins to easily turn off Teams Connect Chat, or limit chat based on select organizations or Teams accounts

2) Limit the number of messages (10) you can send to a new user without a response
We ran three different sessions of user testing and flash feedback through our design process, testing with Figma prototypes.

Microsoft Teams announced the Teams Connect Chat feature during its annual Ignite Conference in 2021. The feature has now been rolled out successfully to the majority of our public users and organizations (with IT admin approval)

As of 2022, Teams Connect Chat has hundreds of thousands of monthly active users, and millions of messages are sent across work and personal accounts each month.

Teams Connect Chat has also enabled several other key investments from the Teams organization, including Parent Connection, SMB (small and medium-sized business) plans, and the Teams Chat pinned app in Windows 11.

/projects/teams-edu.title: Teams EDU | Justin Luo
/projects/teams-edu.description: Class Home Page for Teams EDU
p:
How might we...

Create a "home" experience for class teams that is authentic to education scenarios?

Lead designer for educator and student scenarios on the core feature team. 

Collaborated across three partner teams and worked with PM’s, engineers, researchers, and writers.

Microsoft Teams is used as a learning platform by millions of educators and students around the world, and our goal is to help them create meaningful online learning experiences.

Especially during the COVID pandemic, educators relied on Teams and other digital learning tools to teach students.

Through our customer feedback channels, we saw potential to improve the current class team experience.

In its current state, the General channel was not an authentic "home" experience for class teams:

✗  Teachers can’t customize the layout
✗  Posts don’t offer enough flexibility in content
✗  Hard to find important information in a feed
With our user research team, we conducted interviews with 100 teachers, split evenly among elementary, middle school, high school, and education.

We saw an opportunity to build a new "home" dashboard to:

✔  Increase customer engagement in class teams
✔  Help students and educators prioritize and find important information

We also saw an opportunity to collaborate cross-org with Microsoft SharePoint, a website-building platform with several existing widgets.
To kick off our collaborations, the Teams EDU + SharePoint teams conducted a design jam together, hoping to set the stage for the project direction and goals.
From this design jam, we defined three key principles:
With SharePoint’s expertise in site building and customization, we pushed forward with the idea of a home dashboard powered by SharePoint pages and web parts.

How should a home dashboard be built into class teams?

After exploring various models, we eventually decided between two frontrunners.

Ultimately, we decided to go with Model 1, since it was both discoverable and utilized our existing framework that customers understood. We also did not want to confuse customers who already used Posts in the General channel.

We agreed that we should revisit the idea of setting the “Home” class app as the default landing experience over the General channel after the initial release.

How do we provide value off the bat?

To start, we wanted to leverage SharePoint’s existing web parts to automatically pull information from the class including files, assignments, and calendar events.

From SharePoint’s previous data, we also knew that customer engagement increased when starting from a site template rather than from scratch.

Referencing our research study on class dashboards, we proposed a new class home template:

How do we make this feel like one cohesive product?

Even if we were using a SharePoint page as the home dashboard for a class team, we wanted to make sure the experience felt native to Teams.

To do so, we aligned visual and font styles, fixed site interactions, and created custom empty states and help resources.
Bringing SharePoint components and web parts to the latest Fluent design guidelines
Updating interactions to match native Teams patterns, like opening meetings in Teams client instead of the web browser
We ran three different sessions of user testing and flash feedback through our design process, testing with Figma prototypes.
From our web part ranking exercise, we were also able to iterate on the placement of web parts in the default template.

Microsoft Education announced the Class Home Page feature in the summer of 2022 and is now generally available to schools. Since its release, the feature has been used weekly by millions of teachers and students.

One of our team's goals in 2022 was to build EDU authenticity - to meet teachers and students where they are. 

We believe that the Class Home Page was a great first step in that direction, and we were incredibly happy that the feature was met with such positive response.

/.title: Justin Luo
p: With 5+ years of product design experience, I've built products that have impacted millions of peoples' lives. I care deeply about about craft, product excellence, and the impact of my work. Currently, I'm designing tools for creators at Patreon. Previously, I've designed products at Prepared and Microsoft Teams.

I'm a designer by trade, but I'm most passionate about building great products, whether that's creating pixel-perfect mocks, writing code, or project planning. Right now, I design tools to help creators build their businesses at Patreon. Before that, I was the founding designer at Prepared, a startup focused on innovating 911 emergency response technology. I joined Prepared as employee #15 and led product design as the company grew from seed to Series B. Previously, I've also designed for the Teams Education product at Microsoft Teams.

Once upon a time, I studied Computer Science and Human-Centered Design at  Dartmouth College. That's where I discovered my passion for design while developing the technical skills that allow me to bridge design and engineering today.
